exemplar.title: How Does Jane Austen Use Unconventional Relationships to Express Her Attitude Towards Love and Marriage and to Criticize the Role of Women in Pride and Prejudice?

detailedExemplarData.title: How Does Jane Austen Use Unconventional Relationships to Express Her Attitude Towards Love and Marriage and to Criticize the Role of Women in Pride and Prejudice?

subject.title: English A Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **literary texts from diverse cultures, genres, and time periods**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis, interpretation, and understanding of how literature reflects and shapes human experience through themes, cultural contexts, and global issues**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Unseen literary text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay on studied works | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 40% |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation on global issues | 10 minutes | 10 minutes | 25% |

strengths: Clear, focused research question stated prominently and sustained throughout the essay
Consistent thematic signposting via well-labelled sections maintains reader orientation
Title and introduction succinctly communicate scope and purpose
weaknesses: Methodology is implied rather than explicitly justified, reducing methodological transparency
Occasional digressions into biographical and historical material slightly loosen the focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs Topic Communication and Research Question meet the top of the 5â6 band, with precise framing and clear purpose. Methodological choices are generally effective but lack explicit rationale, and a few tangents weaken full consistency. On balance, the work demonstrates strong but not flawless focus and method, meriting 5/6.

strengths: Accurate and consistent deployment of literary-critical terminology underpinning substantive claims
Effective integration of a range of secondary scholarly sources to contextualize and support analysis
Detailed knowledge of text demonstrated through nuanced close readings
weaknesses: Occasional overgeneralization in historical context and narrower scope (focus on middle/upper class women) limit comprehensiveness
Minor lapses in conceptual precision (e.g., unexplained feminist theory moments)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs use of terminology and integration of sources are characteristic of the 5â6 band, and knowledge of Pride and Prejudice is strong. Occasional imprecision and a somewhat narrowed contextual scope prevent a full 6, so a score of 5/6 accurately reflects solid but not uniformly exhaustive knowledge and understanding.

strengths: Substantial, relevant peer-reviewed research consistently tied to the research question
Sustained close readings that move beyond description into analysis of narrative voice, irony, and gender dynamics
Coherent argumentative progression from conventional to unconventional relationships
weaknesses: Some analytical claims (e.g., feminism links, legal commentary) lack full evidential support
Evaluation of scholarly perspectives is more affirmative than critically interrogative, and counter-arguments are underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality sits comfortably in the 7â9 band, analysis in the 4â6 band and discussion in the 4â6 band. While the essay demonstrates strong, relevant research and generally effective analysis, lapses in evidence support and limited critical evaluation constrain the top half of the scale. A balanced overall mark of 7/12 reflects the paperâs solid critical thinking with room for deeper evaluation.

strengths: All required structural elements present (title page, contents, introduction, conclusion, works cited) and clearly organized
Section headings and logical progression enhance navigability
Standard formatting (pagination, spacing, referencing) consistently applied
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in bibliography spacing and occasional overly long paragraphs reduce polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation fully meets the descriptors of Band 3â4 with clear structure and correct formatting, aside from minor typographical polish issues. Awarding the top band score of 4/4 appropriately reflects the strength of the presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay shows a mostly complete method: close literary analysis combined with consultation of secondary criticism (journal articles, monographs, reference guides).  
â¢ Source selection is generally relevant and varied, yet a handful of popular aids (e.g., CliffsNotes) sit uneasily beside peer-reviewed scholarship, signalling only partial selectivity.  
â¢ Methodological explanation is implicit rather than explicit; the student describes the plan in the introduction but does not reflect on why specific critical approaches were chosen, so the effectiveness is adequate rather than consistently compellingâcharacteristic of Band 3-4.

Essential layout conventionsâtitle, word count, pagination, double spacing, standard font, MLA referencingâare all in place.  
â¢ There are slight inconsistencies in bibliography spacing and some lengthy unbroken paragraphs, which detract from the overall polish.  
â¢ Because these issues are relatively minor and most requirements are correctly applied, the work earns a solid but not maximum score within Band 3-4 (score 3).

The bibliography lists a substantial range of peer-reviewed journal articles (ELH, *Feminist Studies*, *The Eighteenth Century*) alongside the primary text, demonstrating strong research depth.  
â¢ Selected sources are current, reputable, and clearly relevant to the research question. Citations are generally accurate and integrated into analysis.  
â¢ A small proportion of material (e.g., CliffsNotes) is less scholarly, and there is limited engagement with recent Austen studies post-2010, preventing the work from reaching the very top of the criterion. Hence the lower end of Band 7-9 is appropriate (score 7).

The studentâs topicâAustenâs use of unconventional relationships to critique love, marriage, and womenâs rolesâis stated succinctly on the title page and reiterated in the introduction.  
â¢ The opening paragraph sets a clear historical frame (Regency society, 19th-century marriage conventions) and identifies the literary text under investigation.  
â¢ Throughout the essay, section headings (âSocial Conventions and Marriage,â âThe Gray Areaâ¦,â âUnconventional Marriagesâ¦â) maintain a consistent thematic focus, confirming that the purpose is well signposted and never in doubt.  
Given the precision, consistency, and reader-friendly organisation, the strand meets the descriptors for Band 5-6 and merits the top of that band.

The discussion is coherent, progressing from conventional to unconventional marriages and culminating in a reflective conclusion that revisits the research question.  
â¢ Evaluation of scholarly perspectives (e.g., Moe, Newton) is present but often affirmed rather than critically weighed; counter-arguments (e.g., alternative feminist readings) are acknowledged only briefly.  
â¢ The conclusion recognises limitations but stops short of proposing substantive implications or future research directions. This positions the work mid-to-high within Band 4-6 (score 5).

The essay offers sustained close readings (CharlotteâCollins, LizzyâDarcy, Lady Catherine) and usually secures claims with textual or critical evidence.  
â¢ Analysis moves beyond description, exploring narrative voice, irony, and gender dynamics; however, some arguments (e.g., links to 1960s feminism, entailment laws) are asserted rather than demonstrated, and occasional repetition dilutes analytical precision.  
â¢ Given the generally strong but not uniformly incisive analysis, the work best fits the top of Band 4-6 (score 6).

The report contains all required structural elementsâtitle page, abstract substitute (word count), contents page, introduction, structured chapters, conclusion, and works cited.  
â¢ Logical progression from context to analysis to conclusion is clear; section headings aid navigation.  
â¢ Minor issues (occasional overly long paragraphs, some uneven subsection balance) do not undermine the overall appropriateness, justifying placement in Band 3-4 at the top score.

The student demonstrates good knowledge of *Pride and Prejudice* and of relevant criticism. Close readings are detailed (e.g., analysis of CharlotteâCollins dialogues, Lady Catherineâs diction) and secondary sources are integrated to contextualise points.  
â¢ However, some generalisations about historical context (e.g., blanket claims about womenâs possibilities) remain only partially evidenced, and the focus is almost exclusively on middle- and upper-class women, narrowing the scope of understanding.  
â¢ Overall comprehension is strong but not uniformly nuanced or comprehensive, aligning with Band 3-4.

Literary-critical terminologyâ"free indirect discourse," "metonymy," "omniscient narrator," "narrative structure"âis deployed accurately and with confidence.  
â¢ Terms are not merely name-checked; they underpin substantive analytical claims (e.g., linking free indirect discourse to narrative voice and gendered critique).  
â¢ Minor lapses in precision occur, but overall usage is both **accurate** and **consistent**, meriting Band 5-6 (score 5 rather than 6 owing to the occasional missed opportunity to clarify concepts such as anachronism or feminist theory).

The research question is explicitly formulated and appears on the title page. It is generally focused on Austenâs deployment of âunconventional relationshipsâ to convey attitudes toward marriage and womenâs roles.  
â¢ The body chapters return to this question, but at times the discussion drifts into biographical speculation (Austenâs own life) and tangential historical commentary (1960s Womenâs Movement) that only partially advance the line of inquiry.  
â¢ Consequently, while the question is clear and mostly appropriate, the sustained connection between every analytical move and the research question is not fully consistent, placing the work solidly in Band 3-4.

annotations.comment: The examination of free indirect discourse and metonymy demonstrates precise application of literary concepts to support claims about narrative voice and gender critique.
The discussion on narrative structure drifts into description rather than evaluating how the structure advances the argument; more explicit connection to the research question is needed.
The research question is stated clearly and concisely, orienting the inquiry toward Austenâs use of unconventional relationships to critique marriage and womenâs roles.
The word count is documented and meets the 4000-word limit, satisfying the formatting requirement.
The table of contents is well-organised with accurate page references, enhancing navigability and presentation.
The introduction provides strong historical context for Regency marriage conventions, demonstrating good knowledge of the topic and sources.
The introduction clearly outlines the essayâs three main argumentative strands, effectively planning the analytical focus and structure.
The integration of Barclay and Stanworthâs criticism enhances contextual understanding and demonstrates effective use of varied sources.
The analysis of Charlotte Lucasâs pragmatic marriage shows depth of critical engagement, linking character interaction to economic pressures in society.
The discussion occasionally reiterates plot summary without advancing interpretation; the student should focus on explaining how each example supports the research question.
The title precisely communicates the focus on social conventions, marriage, and love in Pride and Prejudice, establishing clear purpose and scope for the investigation.
This paragraph tends toward summary of earlier analysis; the student should use this space to critically evaluate implications rather than recap.
Section headers maintain logical progression and clarity, aiding the reader in following the shift to gray-area analysis.
The claim that Lady Catherineâs marriage proposal to Anne is incestuous is overstated and lacks textual support; more nuanced evidence is required.
The opening of the unconventional marriages section clearly refocuses on Darcy and Elizabeth, maintaining alignment with the research question.
The contents page heading and layout are clear, guiding the reader through the structure of the report effectively.
The methodology is implied rather than explicitly described; the student should explain why close reading and selected secondary sources were chosen to strengthen methodological transparency.
Reliance on Austenâs biography risks shifting focus from textual analysis; the student should more tightly link biographical details to textual evidence for relevance.
The satirical depiction of Mr. Collins and Mr. Bennet reflects strong critical thinking and textual support, illustrating power dynamics in marriage conventions.
The discussion of hyperbole and irony in Collinsâs portrayal uses literary terminology accurately, enhancing the analysis of Austenâs narrative techniques.
The conclusion of this section effectively synthesizes how unconventional couples overshadow conventional marriages, reinforcing the essayâs thematic progression.
The analysis of Jane and Bingleyâs relationship highlights the complexity of conventionality versus emotional intent, showing effective use of textual evidence.
This section over-describes plot events without critically linking examples to the research question; focus on interpretation would deepen the argument.
Close reading of the âmaster of the houseâ metonymy and Bingleyâs restraint demonstrates accurate and consistent use of literary concepts.
Extensive quotations dominate this paragraph; the student should pair each quotation with deeper analysis to justify its relevance.
